qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Uncontrolled recursion in BER parser causes denial of service via stack overflow
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: Different vulnerable code and root cause: this finding targets uncontrolled recursion in pkcs7/ber.go readObject() via pkcs7.Parse(), while the advisory targets SM9 ciphertext forgery from missing rejection of the infinity point C1 in sm9.UnwrapKey/Decrypt.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No final qualifying finding identifies the advisory-described SM9 infinity-point ciphertext forgery root cause. The only qualifying finding is a PKCS#7 BER parser denial-of-service issue in a different component with a different source-to-sink path and security consequence.
evidence: Advisory GHSA-5xxp-2vrj-x855 describes SM9 decryption accepting C1 as the point at infinity because UnwrapKey/Decrypt only deserialize and check on-curve status without explicitly rejecting the group identity.
The advisory impact is ciphertext forgery: a forged SM9 ciphertext with all-zero/infinity C1 decrypts successfully to attacker-chosen plaintext and passes the C3 integrity check.
ber_recursion_dos/vulnerability_report.json describes pkcs7.Parse() calling ber2der() and recursively entering pkcs7/ber.go readObject() for deeply nested BER SEQUENCE objects until stack exhaustion.

The BER recursion finding does not reference sm9.Decrypt, sm9.DecryptASN1, sm9.UnwrapKey, C1 validation, point-at-infinity handling, pairing degeneration, KDF predictability, or SM9 ciphertext forgery.
blockers_to_higher_tier: No advisory-matched qualifying finding was reported, so Tier1/Tier2/Tier3 classification is not applicable.
